text: In my digital scientific notation Leibniz (<https://github.com/khinsen/leibniz>), I have tried to imitate the habits of the mathematical/scientific literature: every text introduces its whole notation explicitly, without any defaults nor any reuse of notation from other texts. If you want to write anything in Leibniz, you have to compose your linguistic context explicitly. There are no hierarchical namespaces either, intentionally, to discourage the building of large reusable libraries that nobody can fully understand anymore.

text:
My lesson learned over many years is this: the things we are all proposing here are hard enough in their substance to even explain to others, let alone persuade them. We really have no leeway to be unique snowflakes about minor details of presentation. You really need to preserve your readers' cognitive resources for the core argument you're making.

Put another way: if I see something weird in your doc, it better be super critical to your doc. Otherwise I'm definitely gonna bounce.

The name of the game is becoming hard to dismiss.

text:
languages which can overwrite code like Lisp can of course overload symbols, and is well known to be one of the most flexible languages around. Postscript also allows for redefinition of symbols freely. But the king of brevity in terms of languages is perennially FORTH, whose programmers take delight in rewriting the meaning of + temporarily, and the results are incredibly impressive in both speed and compactness. The problem with FORTH is that it is basically a write-only language, and it is super hard to understand your own code a month later, much less other people's code, because you have to keep in your mind what is on the stack, and how many operators are consumed by each operand. Another language which is highly flexible is Red/Rebol.

In my efforts to develop my Beads language i have moved in the complete opposite direction so that any feature which makes things harder to read is removed, and the whole thrust is to make programs minimal while promoting clarity. For example + in JS means add OR could mean string concat; i use + for add, and & for concat so you can't mix them by mistake (or cause ruinous implied type conversions which JS does so readily).

text:
<@UCUSW7WVD> the `[+]` I believe means that the editor buffer is changed. You can only see it when I am in insert mode because the video covers it otherwise. The tool only knows about the file, it polls for modifications, evaluates it and writes back the re-stringified AST.

I guess for presenting this project, I should maybe use a more mainstream editor, because the terminal view does potentially alienate those not familiar with e.g. vim. I also need to find a way to communicate better when exactly the file is saved, it's a bit hard to tell in the video because I just type the command very fast.

<@UE3KC0VK6>: It might be more powerful, but I don't think in metaprogramming: as I understand, RxJS *runs* JS to create the expression tree. So basically you have a turing-complete preprocessor. I don't have enough primitives for eval-time turing completeness, but that should just be a matter of time as far as I can tell. The differences to Obervable as far as I can tell are two:

1.) My language has explicitly defined semantics and UX for updating the graph at runtime. This is the main contribution I think, I'm not expecting to come up with a better general purpose FRP language here.
2.) The metaprogramming and flowbased logic have the same syntax and share some semantics. The metaprogramming only runs at eval-time, but at eval-time a single tick of flow-logic is also executed incrementally as the tree is built. Due to the dependency tracking, it is also known whether values are dependent on the root event or not. Based on this, the metaprogramming operations can access computation results, and determine whether values are subject to future changes or not. So (+ 3 3) is an eval-time constant, while (+ 3 (time/count 1)) is not (time/count has an implicit  dependency on the root event). It's essentially constant folding.

event streams are 'dirty' even if the value is the same as the previous one (e.g. playing the note C then some time later the note C again - two events). Between events the stream is 'empty'. On the other hand value streams are always at a value, and may even want to be interpolated. They don't need to be marked dirty when the new value is the old, and reassigning an input from one value stream to another shouldn't be considered a change when the values are the same either.

text: right now the root event clocks about every millisecond, then everything time-clocked gets a delta event from a clock, which slows that down to 60fps
right now the root event clocks about every millisecond, then everything time-clocked gets a delta event from a clock, which slows that down to 60fps

so the root event is completely independent from time, so it can run whenever it wants to, it just needs to run at least "often enough" to make whatever you're doing work. How much fidelity you need you can configure in your clock instance.

In theory the root event doesn't have to be a time-based loop (only), it could also wake up on other events that operators hinted at (e.g. incoming MIDI messages) for tighter timing.

Interesting approach. I think this is the key aspect:
>  the identity of individual expressions is tracked using tags
Essentially you have objects with identity and corresponding mini-views in the editor. It's clever how this is embedded in a typical text editing experience using tags. Would definitely play around with it more once you publish 'getting started' or similar.

text: <@UNGNT3BKR> I've been wondering something.  In your system you use tags to remember expressions; but in something like extempore he does not .  I assume (from what I've observed) that his system does know when you have modified an expression that is already playing - i.e. you don't get a duplicate event, you modify the existing one.  Otherwise all of his 'metro' expressions would end up multiplying, if that makes sense.  So I'm wondering how that works?

text: <@UUQ2EQW21> as far as I understand its about naming there, yes. The 'temporal recursion' basically means that every iteration the binding is resolved again and whatever is the new value for it takes over. Unlike alive that wont preserve any local state, but you can put the state in a global storage place I guess. I haven't really played with extempore myself though.
